title: Slow Down Before Arguing

segments.boardPlan: Write: question, terms, claim, evidence, repair.
segments.transcript: Clear reasoning begins by slowing down. Before we argue, we identify the question, define the terms, separate the claim from the evidence, and leave room to repair the thought.

segments.boardPlan: Draw a distinction ladder from vague word to stable definition.
segments.transcript: A distinction ladder prevents confusion from hiding inside one word. We test examples, borderline cases, and counterexamples until the term becomes usable.

segments.overlay: Pause and write one disputed word, two possible meanings, and one fair question.
segments.transcript: Pause here. Write one disputed word, two possible meanings, and one fair question you could ask before arguing.
Discipline and charity make reasoning stronger. We do not weaken the opposing view; we make it clear enough to answer honestly.

practicePrompts: Build a distinction ladder for one contested term.
Rewrite a weak opposing argument into a stronger and fairer form before answering it.
